patterns.rootCause: The identifier '{identifier}' is used but has not been declared in the current scope. It may be a missing #include, an undeclared variable, or a typo.
patterns.prescription: Declare '{identifier}' before use, add the appropriate #include header, or check for typos. If it is from the std namespace, ensure 'using namespace std;' or prefix with 'std::'.

patterns.rootCause: A semicolon is missing at the end of a statement or declaration. In C/C++, every statement must end with a semicolon.
patterns.prescription: Add a semicolon at the end of the indicated line. Also check for missing semicolons after class/struct definitions (before the closing brace).

patterns.match: error:.*no matching function for call to '([\w:]+)'
patterns.rootCause: No overload of '{funcName}' matches the provided arguments. The argument types, count, or const-qualifiers may be incorrect.
patterns.prescription: Check the function signature of '{funcName}' and adjust the arguments to match. Verify implicit conversions are valid. If it is a template function, ensure template arguments can be deduced correctly.

patterns.rootCause: Cannot convert from '{fromType}' to '{toType}'. There is no implicit conversion between these types.
patterns.prescription: Add an explicit cast using static_cast<{toType}>(value). If converting between pointer types, ensure the types are related. Avoid reinterpret_cast unless absolutely necessary.

patterns.rootCause: '{name}' has been defined more than once. This usually happens when a header file is included multiple times without include guards.
patterns.prescription: Add include guards (#ifndef/#define/#endif) or #pragma once to the header file. Ensure the definition appears in only one source (.cpp) file, with a declaration in the header.

patterns.rootCause: The type '{typeName}' is not recognized. The header defining it may not be included, or it may be in a namespace that has not been specified.
patterns.prescription: Add the #include for the header that defines '{typeName}'. If it is in a namespace, use the fully qualified name (e.g., std::{typeName}). Check for forward declaration requirements.

patterns.rootCause: The compiler expected a name (identifier) but found something else. Common causes: extra semicolons, misplaced braces, or using a reserved keyword as an identifier.
patterns.prescription: Check for extra semicolons after #include directives, misplaced braces, or accidentally using a C++ keyword (class, int, etc.) as a variable or function name.

patterns.rootCause: An implicit type conversion may lose data (e.g., converting double to int truncates, or converting 64-bit to 32-bit may lose precision).
patterns.prescription: Use an explicit cast to acknowledge the conversion: static_cast<TargetType>(value). If precision loss is unacceptable, change the target variable's type to match the source.

patterns.rootCause: The linker cannot find the definition of '{symbol}'. The function or variable is declared but not defined in any linked object file or library.
patterns.prescription: Ensure the source file containing the definition of '{symbol}' is compiled and linked. Add the library to your linker flags (-l flag or target_link_libraries in CMake). Check for name mangling issues when mixing C and C++ (use extern "C").
patterns.codeBlock:
# Link the missing library:
g++ main.o -o app -lmylib -lpthread

# CMake:
target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE mylib pthread)

patterns.rootCause: '{symbol}' is defined in more than one translation unit. This often happens when a function or variable definition is placed in a header without 'inline' or include guards.
patterns.prescription: Move the definition to a single .cpp file, or mark it as 'inline' if it must remain in the header. For global variables in headers, use 'inline' (C++17) or 'extern' with a separate definition file.

patterns.rootCause: The header file '{headerFile}' could not be found in any include path. The file may not be installed, or the include path is not configured.
patterns.prescription: Install the library that provides '{headerFile}'. Add the include directory to your compiler flags (-I path) or CMakeLists.txt (include_directories or target_include_directories).
patterns.codeBlock:
# Install the development package:
sudo apt install libboost-dev  # Debian/Ubuntu
brew install boost              # macOS

# Add include path:
g++ -I/usr/local/include main.cpp

# CMake:
find_package(Boost REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(myapp Boost::headers)

patterns.rootCause: The program tried to access memory it does not own. Common causes: dereferencing a null or dangling pointer, buffer overflow, stack overflow from infinite recursion, or accessing freed memory.
patterns.prescription: Compile with -g -fsanitize=address and run under Valgrind or AddressSanitizer to find the exact location. Use smart pointers (std::unique_ptr, std::shared_ptr) instead of raw pointers. Check all pointer dereferences for null.
patterns.codeBlock:
# Compile with AddressSanitizer:
g++ -g -fsanitize=address -o app main.cpp
./app

# Or use Valgrind:
valgrind --leak-check=full ./app

patterns.rootCause: Memory was freed (deleted) twice. This corrupts the heap and can cause crashes. Common causes: calling delete on the same pointer twice, or a destructor freeing memory that was already freed elsewhere.
patterns.prescription: Use smart pointers to manage ownership automatically. Set pointers to nullptr after deletion. Avoid manual new/delete; prefer std::vector, std::string, and std::unique_ptr.

patterns.rootCause: A buffer overflow was detected on the stack. The program wrote past the end of a stack-allocated buffer, corrupting the stack canary. This is often caused by writing past array bounds or unbounded string operations.
patterns.prescription: Replace fixed-size arrays with std::vector or std::array. Use bounds-checked functions (strncpy instead of strcpy, snprintf instead of sprintf). Compile with -fsanitize=address to pinpoint the exact overflow.

patterns.match: CMake Error.*[Cc]ould not find (?:a )?package (?:configuration file|provided by) ['"]?([\w-]+)
patterns.rootCause: CMake could not find the package '{packageName}'. The development library is not installed, or CMake does not know where to find it.
patterns.prescription: Install the development package for '{packageName}'. Set CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH to the installation directory if it is in a non-standard location. Use 'cmake --find-package' to debug.
patterns.codeBlock:
# Install the package:
sudo apt install lib{packageName}-dev  # Debian/Ubuntu
brew install {packageName}              # macOS

# Or set the search path:
cmake -DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH=/usr/local ..

patterns.rootCause: The type '{typeName}' does not have a member named '{memberName}'. This often appears in template instantiation errors where a type does not satisfy the template's requirements.
patterns.prescription: Check that the type used with the template provides the required member '{memberName}'. For C++20 concepts, verify the type satisfies the concept constraints. Review the template documentation for type requirements.

patterns.match: warning:.*delete called on.*that has virtual functions but non-virtual destructor
patterns.rootCause: A class with virtual functions is being deleted through a base pointer, but the base class destructor is not virtual. This causes undefined behavior and memory leaks.
patterns.prescription: Add a virtual destructor to the base class. Any class intended to be used polymorphically (with virtual functions) should have a virtual destructor.
